full_response:
Let us label the events in Alice’s clock–time, choosing the instant she emits the signal to be  
t′ = 0.

1. “Send’’ (Alice emits the light) : (t′ , θ′) = (0 , θ′_A).  
2. “Receive–reflect’’ (Bob receives and immediately sends it back) : (t′ , θ′) = (dt′₁ , θ′_A+dθ′).  
3. “Return’’ (Alice receives the echo) : (t′ , θ′) = (dt′₁+dt′₂ , θ′_A).

------------------------------------------------
Alice’s halfway event  
Alice takes the mid–point between the instants she sent and re-received the pulse, i.e.

t′_half,A = ½(0 + dt′₁ + dt′₂) = (dt′₁ + dt′₂)/2 ,

and of course it occurs at her position θ′ = θ′_A.

 Coordinates of Alice’s halfway point:
  (t′ , θ′) = ( (dt′₁+dt′₂)/2 , θ′_A ).

------------------------------------------------
Bob’s halfway event  
For Bob the natural “halfway’’ moment is the instant he receives the light from Alice, event 2.  
Hence, before any adjustment of clocks,

 Coordinates of Bob’s halfway point:
  (t′ , θ′) = ( dt′₁ , θ′_A + dθ′ ).

------------------------------------------------
Required time-lag between the clocks  
If the two halfway points are to be the very same space-time event, their time coordinates must agree.  Therefore Bob’s clock has to differ from Alice’s by an amount Δt′ such that

dt′₁ − Δt′ = (dt′₁ + dt′₂)/2 .

Solving,

Δt′ = (dt′₁ − dt′₂)/2 .

------------------------------------------------
Result

\boxed{%
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alice’s halfway event:}&\quad (t′ ,θ′)=\bigl( (dt′_{1}+dt′_{2})/2,\; θ′_{A}\bigr),\\[2pt]
\text{Bob’s halfway event:}&\quad (t′ ,θ′)=\bigl( dt′_{1},\; θ′_{A}+dθ′\bigr),\\[4pt]
\text{required time lag:}&\quad dt′=\dfrac{dt′_{1}-dt′_{2}}{2}.
\end{aligned}}

Confidence: 75%
